section.title: The Plague of Boils
section.sectionRationale: A brief, sharply focused sixth plague, from Moses tossing soot into the air before Pharaoh to the striking detail that even Egypt's magicians, who had copied earlier signs, cannot stand before Moses because they too are afflicted.
section.thesis: The very magicians who once matched God's signs are now themselves covered in boils and unable even to appear before Moses, and here for the first time the text says plainly that the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, without any mention of Pharaoh's own hardening alongside it.
section.frameNote: One scene: soot tossed into the sky becomes festering boils on man and beast, striking down even Egypt's magicians, and the LORD hardens Pharaoh's heart.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses and Aaron, “Take handfuls of soot from the furnace; in the sight of Pharaoh, Moses is to toss it into the air.
It will become fine dust over all the land of Egypt, and festering {{shechin|boils}} will break out on man and beast throughout the land.”
So they took soot from the furnace and stood before Pharaoh. Moses tossed it into the air, and festering boils broke out on man and beast.
The magicians could not stand before Moses, because the boils had broken out on them and on all the Egyptians.
But the LORD {{chazaq3|hardened}} Pharaoh's heart, and he would not listen to them, just as the LORD had said to Moses.

shechin.gloss: Boils, an inflamed, festering skin eruption, the same word used of Job's affliction in Job 2:7 and later listed among the covenant curses in Deuteronomy 28:27. A painful, humiliating disease that strikes indiscriminately at man and beast, high and low.

chazaq3.gloss: To harden or strengthen. This is the first plague in the cycle where the text states plainly and solely that the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, with no parallel statement here of Pharaoh hardening his own heart, sharpening the mystery of divine sovereignty in his resistance.

basic.context: This is the sixth plague, and it is short but pointed. Moses throws soot into the air, and it becomes festering boils on every person and animal in Egypt, so severe that even Pharaoh's own magicians, who had matched earlier signs, cannot even stand before Moses because they too are covered in sores.
basic.hermeneutics: This is real history, and this passage contains one of the clearest single statements in the whole cycle that the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, without pairing it here with any mention of Pharaoh hardening his own heart in this same verse. The passage does not try to soften or explain away this tension; it simply states it.
basic.originalLanguages: The shechin, boils, is the same affliction that struck Job and later appears among the covenant curses in Deuteronomy 28. Here it strikes down even the magicians who had imitated earlier plagues, and the text says the LORD chazaq, hardened, Pharaoh's heart directly.
basic.typology: The magicians who once matched God's power now cannot even stand before His servant, a small picture of how every rival power, however impressive at first, eventually collapses before the greater power of God, a pattern completed when every knee bows before Christ, Philippians 2:10.
basic.christ: The boils, later listed among the curses for covenant unfaithfulness in Deuteronomy 28:27, anticipate how Christ bears the curse of the law for His people, Galatians 3:13, taking on Himself the full weight of judgment that sin deserves so that believers are spared it.
basic.god: God is sovereign even over Pharaoh's hardened will, and this passage does not shy from stating that sovereignty starkly. Yet He remains just, since Pharaoh had already, repeatedly and freely, chosen hardness in the plagues before this one; God's hardening here confirms rather than originates a direction Pharaoh had already set.
basic.crossReferences: Job 2:7 describes Job's own affliction with the same word for boils. Deuteronomy 28:27 lists boils among the curses for covenant disobedience. Romans 9:18 discusses God's freedom in showing mercy and hardening.
basic.oneStory: This is a sharp, almost humiliating strike in God's judgment on Egypt, striking down even its magicians, and stating plainly that the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart. It sharpens the mystery of divine sovereignty and human responsibility that Paul later addresses directly in Romans 9, a tension the Bible holds without resolving.

context.literary: The sixth and shortest plague narrative apart from the gnats, marked by the total disappearance of the magicians from the story after this point, their earlier role as counterfeit rivals now finally and completely ended by their own suffering.
context.historical: Egyptian medical and religious texts show great concern with skin disease and its treatment, often invoking deities associated with healing; a painful, visible, and widespread skin affliction striking indiscriminately would have been read as both physically devastating and religiously humiliating for a culture that prized ritual purity and bodily wholeness.
context.canonical: The disease here, shechin, links this plague to Job's suffering, Job 2:7, and to the covenant curses later listed in Deuteronomy 28:27, tying the plague narrative directly into the broader biblical theology of covenant blessing and curse.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Egypt's counterfeit power has now been completely silenced, even physically incapacitated, and to state directly, without qualification in this verse, that the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes a specific historical judgment and a specific instance of divine hardening within a unique redemptive-historical moment; it is not a general statement that God hardens every hardened heart in exactly the same way. The enduring truth is that God's sovereignty over even the most resistant human will is real, and Scripture states it without apology even when it does not fully explain it.

debates.question: Does this verse, with no mention of Pharaoh's own hardening, tip the balance toward divine determinism over human responsibility?

views.label: This verse must be read within the whole cycle
views.case: Earlier verses, 8:15 and 8:32, explicitly record Pharaoh hardening his own heart; this verse's silence on that point does not erase the pattern established earlier, but continues a single, complex reality already described from both angles.

views.label: The hardening intensifies as judgment proceeds
views.case: Some read a deliberate escalation across the cycle, where after enough self-hardening, God's hardening in response takes a more decisive, unilateral form, a judicial confirming of Pharaoh in the path he had already chosen.

debates.bearing: Both views affirm that Pharaoh remains genuinely responsible for his sustained pattern of resistance, even as God's sovereign purpose stands behind and beneath that resistance.

study.originalLanguages: The shechin, boils, is the same word used for Job's affliction and later listed among covenant curses in Deuteronomy 28:27. Here the text states directly that the LORD chazaq, hardened, Pharaoh's heart, a stark, unqualified statement of divine sovereignty over his continued resistance.

typology.fulfillment: A picture of every rival power eventually collapsing before God's greater power, fulfilled when every knee bows and every tongue confesses Jesus Christ is Lord, Philippians 2:10-11.
Anticipates Christ bearing the curse of the law for His people, becoming a curse for us, so that believers are redeemed from every covenant curse listed in Deuteronomy 28, Galatians 3:13.

study.christ: The boils that strike Egypt are later listed explicitly among the curses for covenant unfaithfulness, Deuteronomy 28:27, tying this plague to the broader theology of blessing and curse that Christ ultimately resolves by becoming a curse for us, redeeming His people from the curse of the law, Galatians 3:13. Just as Egypt's magicians are finally silenced here, every power opposed to God will one day be silenced before Christ, Philippians 2:10-11.
study.god: God's sovereignty over Pharaoh's hardened heart is stated here with striking directness, yet this does not erase the earlier record of Pharaoh's own repeated self-hardening in chapters 8 and 9. God is both perfectly sovereign and perfectly just, confirming a hardened man in the direction he had already, freely, and repeatedly chosen for himself.

crossReferences.note: Uses the same Hebrew word, shechin, for Job's own painful affliction.
Lists boils among the curses for covenant unfaithfulness, tying this plague to later covenant theology.
Paul directly addresses the tension of God's sovereignty in hardening, drawing on this very narrative.
Every knee will bow and every tongue confess Jesus Christ is Lord, the ultimate silencing of every rival power.

study.oneStory: This is a sharp, humbling strike in God's judgment on Egypt, silencing even its magicians and stating directly that the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart. It sharpens one of Scripture's hardest mysteries, God's sovereignty over a resistant human will, a tension the Bible states honestly and that Paul later addresses directly in Romans 9, without ever excusing Pharaoh's own real guilt.

quiz.q: How did Moses initiate this sixth plague?

options.text: He poured water from the Nile onto the ground.
options.note: That action belongs to the first plague, not this one.

options.text: He performed no action at all.
options.note: A specific action, tossing soot into the air, is explicitly described.

options.text: He struck the ground with his staff.
options.note: The staff is used in other plagues, but this specific plague begins with soot tossed into the air.

options.text: He tossed handfuls of soot from a furnace into the air before Pharaoh.
options.note: Verses 8 to 10 describe this exact action commanded by the LORD.

quiz.q: What happened to Pharaoh's magicians during this plague?

options.text: They are not mentioned in this passage.
options.note: Their affliction and inability to stand before Moses is explicitly recorded in verse 11.

options.text: They fled Egypt entirely.
options.note: The text describes them being physically unable to stand before Moses due to the boils, not fleeing the country.

options.text: They could not stand before Moses because the boils had broken out on them too.
options.note: Verse 11 records that the magicians themselves were afflicted and rendered unable to appear before Moses.

options.text: They successfully replicated the sign once again.
options.note: They are not recorded as replicating anything here; instead, they are incapacitated by the same affliction.

quiz.q: What is distinctive about the language of hardening in verse 12 compared to some earlier plagues?

options.text: It says the heart of the magicians was hardened, not Pharaoh's.
options.note: The hardening is specifically attributed to Pharaoh's heart, not the magicians'.

options.text: It says only that Pharaoh hardened his own heart, with no mention of the LORD.
options.note: The text explicitly attributes the hardening to the LORD in this verse, not solely to Pharaoh.

options.text: It states directly that the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, without a parallel mention of Pharaoh hardening his own heart in this verse.
options.note: This directness is notable compared to earlier verses like 8:15 and 8:32, which explicitly describe Pharaoh hardening his own heart.

options.text: No hardening language appears in this passage.
options.note: Hardening language is explicit and central to verse 12.

quiz.q: What other biblical passages use the same Hebrew word for boils found in this plague?

options.text: No other passage uses this same word.
options.note: The word shechin appears meaningfully in both Job 2:7 and Deuteronomy 28:27.

options.text: This word appears only once in the entire Bible.
options.note: The same word appears in at least Job 2:7 and Deuteronomy 28:27, among other places.

options.text: Job's own affliction in Job 2:7 and the list of covenant curses in Deuteronomy 28:27.
options.note: Both passages use the same Hebrew word, shechin, tying this plague to broader biblical themes of suffering and covenant curse.

options.text: Only the plagues of Revelation use this word.
options.note: While Revelation describes similar afflictions, the specific Hebrew word shechin connects most directly to Job and Deuteronomy.

quiz.q: What broader theological tension does this brief plague narrative sharpen?

options.text: This passage raises no significant theological tension.
options.note: This is widely recognized as one of the sharper statements of divine sovereignty in the entire hardening theme.

options.text: Whether Egypt actually worshiped multiple gods.
options.note: While Egyptian polytheism is relevant background to the plagues generally, this specific passage centers on the hardening language, not a survey of Egyptian religion.

options.text: The exact chemical composition of the boils.
options.note: The text is not concerned with a scientific or medical explanation of the affliction.

options.text: The mystery of God's sovereignty in hardening a heart alongside genuine human responsibility for sin.
options.note: The stark statement that the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, without qualification here, intensifies this tension that Paul later addresses in Romans 9.

questions: The magicians who once matched God's power were now unable even to stand before Moses. What rival sources of confidence in your own life eventually prove powerless before God?

This passage states plainly that the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, a hard truth Scripture does not soften. How do you hold together trust in God's sovereignty with the real responsibility you carry for your own choices?

Boils later appear among the curses for covenant unfaithfulness in Deuteronomy 28. How does knowing Christ bore the curse of the law for you change how you view painful seasons of discipline or consequence?

Even Egypt's most skilled and resistant advisers were finally silenced by this plague. Where do you need to trust that every opposing voice or power in your life will ultimately be silenced before God?

This is one of the plainer statements of divine hardening in all of Scripture, and it is not explained away. What hard truth about God have you been avoiding wrestling with honestly in your own faith?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew shechin, boils, describes an inflamed, festering skin eruption, the same term used of Job's suffering in Job 2:7 and later listed among the curses for covenant unfaithfulness in Deuteronomy 28:27.

This is the first plague where the text states the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart without a parallel clause describing Pharaoh hardening his own heart in the same verse, a detail much discussed in the theological debate over divine sovereignty and human responsibility.
